content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
[ADDRESS TEXT - 921 ... 110 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814]

October 3, 1976

Public C. [illegible]
Advisory for [illegible] Committee
on Civil Rights
Avenue
CA 92350

Re: Request for Opinion
No. 76-012

Dear [illegible]:

This letter is to advise you that at its October [illegible] the Fair Political Practices Commission instructed us to send you this letter in lieu of the formal opinion you requested (No. 76-012). Since accepting your request last month, legislation has been enacted which appears to add new interpretation you asked and the Commission has adopted [illegible] which affect its dispositive with respect to the second [illegible]. Consequently, your request to lower raises a substantial question or interpretation of the Political Reform Act.

Your first question asked whether the Ad Hoc Committee on Chicano Rights (hereinafter the "Ad Hoc Committee") is a "committee," as defined in Government Code Section [illegible]), when it raised more than $500 to support a recall. The Ad Hoc Committee was not the proponent of the recall. You were given interim advice that the Ad Hoc Committee was not a committee by virtue of its activities supporting the recall effort referred to in your letter and that the Commission's opinion, if different from that advice would be given prospective application only.

Since that time, Government Code Section 84202 has been amended to make it clear that persons supporting or opposing the qualification of a measure, as well as the proponent of a measure, must file campaign statements after the measure qualifies or has failed to qualify for the ballot. This amendment will become effective in January, 1977, and, pursuant to it, the Ad Hoc Committee will become a committee within the meaning of the Political Reform Act if, in the future, it solicits $500 or more to support petition circulation in connection with a recall effort (or a referendum or initiative).

(19)

C

[illegible text at top]

...your second question asked whether the Ad Hoc Com-
mittee was involved when it raised over $500 to support
voter registration activities. You stated that the committee's
registration drive is directed at persons who have not previously
registered to vote due to language problems and other social
barriers. In addition, you stated that the committee views
its efforts to register voters as independent from its activities
as part of a recall effort or candidacy for political
[illegible].

Since accepting your opinion request, the Commission
adopted regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215
relating to "contributions." This regulation specifies
that funds received by an organization that is involved in a
prohibited activity in addition to supporting candidates or
[illegible] are "contributions" only if they are "earmarked" for
political purposes. The regulation also provides that a payment
is "earmarked" only if, at the time the payment is made, the
[illegible] or organization has any reason to know that the payment or funds
[illegible] to be commingled will be used by the donee to
support [illegible].

The Ad Hoc Committee is the type of multipurpose
organization to which this regulation is applicable because
the committee carries on a number of activities concerned with
the promotion and protection of the rights of minority groups,
indigents, aliens and undocumented persons. Furthermore, we
do not think that funds received by the Ad Hoc Committee in
connection with voter registration efforts are "earmarked"
within the meaning of the regulation because disbursements by
the Committee for voter registration activities are not contri-
butions or expenditures. The disbursements would be "contribu-
tions" only if they were made to a candidate, a committee
controlled by a candidate, an official committee of a political
party or another organization which existed primarily for
political purposes, or if they were "earmarked" in the manner
previously discussed. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215(b)
and (j). The disbursements would be "expenditures" only if
they were made in connection with a communication which
broadly advocated the election or defeat of a clearly identi-
fied candidate or the passage or defeat of a clearly identified
ballot measure. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18225(d). The
voter registration activities you describe and the disburse-
ments made in connection with them do not fall within either
of these categories. Consequently, the funds raised by the

(                                                           C

     [illegible] Poker in [illegible]
     [illegible]
     [illegible]


     As to committees for voter registration are not "contributions,"
     and the organization did not become a committee by virtue of
     collecting such funds.

     I hope this information will be of assistance. If
     you have any further questions, please contact Natalie E.
     Wisse, an attorney with the Commission's Legal Division.

                                                     Sincerely,

                                                     [signature]

                                                     Michael Bennett
                                                     Executive Director

     [illegible]

1837 Highland Ave.        Nat'l City, Cal. 92050        (714) 477-8820

EXECUTIVE BOARD                     January 29, 1976

Chairman
Herman Baca              Ms. Regina Kutchins
                         Fair Political Practices Commission
Vice Chairman            Technical Assistance and Analysis Division
Albert Puente            1100 Kay Street
                         Sacramento, California, 95814
Secretary
Albert Garcia            RE: INTERPRETATION OF GOVERNMENT CODE SECTION 82013.

Treasurer
Ralph Inzunza            Dear Ms. Kutchins:

                         As per our telephonic conversation of January 29, 1976,
                         I am writing this letter requesting an official response
                         from your office whether or not the Ad Hoc Committee on
                         Chicano Rights is a "committee" for purposes of California
                         Government Code section 82013.

                         The Ad Hoc Committee on Chicano Rights has been in existence
                         for approximately three years. It is made up of a number of
                         directors of local community organizations and various indiv-
                         iduals. The Committee has concerned itself largely with the
                         promotion and protection of the rights of minority groups,
                         indigents, aliens and undocumented·persons.

                         On October 28, 1975, the Committee organized a mass community
                         meeting in National City following the shooting-death of
                         a National City youth by a police officer. Following the
                         shooting incident, the National City Council had refused to
                         issue the name of the officer involved. Various courses of
                         action were adopted at the October 28 meeting including the
                         following: The Committee would seek a Grand Jury investigation
                         into the shooting; the Committee would pursue a federal
                         Civil Rights Commission investigation of the incident; the
                         Committee would pursue an investigation by the State Attorney
                         General's Office into the shooting incident; the Committee
                         would call for the firing of the Chief of Police of National
                         City; the Committee would seek to establish a citizens panel
                         to review the incident; the Committee would organize a
                         recall effort aimed at the members of the National City Council.

                         MEMBER ORGANIZATIONS

C.A.S.A. Justicia-Chicano Federation-G.I. Forum-Hermandad Igualdad de Derechos-M.A.A.C.-M.A.P.A.

Ms. Regina Kutchlas
Page 2.

The Committee raised approximately five-hundred callers at the
mass mailing of October 20, 1975, people donating money without
specifying which particular Committee activity their donation
was to go towards. Subsequently, on November 14, 1975, pursuant
to Elections Code section 27 50¹, certain individuals, who are
also members of the Ad Hoc Committee, filed with the City Clerk
of National City notices of intention to circulate recall
petitions against three members of the National City Council.
The Ad Hoc Committee was not named as a proponent of the recall
effort.

Subsequent thereto, the Ad Hoc Committee did coordinate the
circulation of petitions and raised in excess of five-hundred
dollars for the purpose of financing and advertising it's involve-
ment in the effort to acquire signatures on the recall petitions.
The Ad Hoc Committee has at all times relevant herein had a
treasurer who has maintained records on all donations and all
expenditures of the Committee.

The petitions were turned over to the City Clerk on December 31,
1975, and, according to information that I just received today,
were certified as being non-sufficient as of approximately
January 28, 1976.

Based on the above information could you please inform me as
to whether or not the Ad Hoc Committee on Chicano Rights is deemed
to be a "Committee" for purposes of Government Code section 82013?
Does our Committee, as described above, have any obligations
under the Political Reform Act of 1974, and if so, could you
please outline our obligations thereunder or refer me to the
appropriate statuses.

Thank you for your cooperation in this matter.

Sincerely Yours,

Peter A. Schey
Attorney for Ad Hoc Committee

cc: Susan Baisden, Chief Elections Attorney, Secretary of State,
    Kenneth Brown, District Attorney's Office, San Diego County,
    Robert Denny, San Diego County Registrar of Voters.

PS:ys

embedding.qa_text: **State of California Fair Political Practices Commission** PO BOX 946 - SACRAMENTO, 95804 • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 October 6, 1976 Mr. Peter A. Boney Attorney for Ad Hoc Committee on Chicano Rights 1387 Highland Avenue Los Angeles, CA 92050 Re: Request for Opinion No. 76-015 Mr. Boney: This letter is to advise you that at its October meeting the Fair Political Practices Commission instructed the staff to send you this letter in lieu of the formal opinion you requested (No. 76-015). Since accepting your request for an opinion, legislation has been enacted which responds to the first question you asked and the Commission has adopted regulations which are dispositive with respect to the second question. Consequently, your request no longer raises a substantial question of interpretation of the Political Reform Act. Your first question asked whether the Ad Hoc Committee on Chicano Rights (hereinafter the "Ad Hoc Committee") became a "committee," as defined in Government Code Section 84013(a), when it raised more than $500 to support a recall that failed to qualify for the ballot. The Ad Hoc Committee was not the proponent of the recall. You were given interim advice that the Ad Hoc Committee was not a committee by virtue of its activities supporting the recall effort referred to in your letter and that the Commission's opinion, if different from that advice, would be given prospective application only. Since that time, Government Code Section 84202 has been amended to make it clear that persons supporting or opposing the qualification of a measure, as well as the proponent of a measure, must file campaign statements after the measure has qualified or has failed to qualify for the ballot. This amendment will become effective in January, 1977, and, pursuant to it, the Ad Hoc Committee will become a committee within the meaning of the Political Reform Act if, in the future, it collects $500 or more to support petition circulation in connection with a recall effort (or a referendum or initiative). Your second question asked whether the Ad Hoc Committee became a committee when it raised over $500 to support voter registration activities. You stated that the committee's registration drive is directed at persons who have not previously registered to vote due to language problems and other social problems. In addition, you stated that the committee views its efforts to register voters as independent from its activities in support of a recall effort or candidates for political office. Since accepting your opinion request, the Commission has promulgated regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215 defining the term "contribution." This regulation specifies that funds received by an organization that is involved in a variety of activities in addition to supporting candidates or measures are "contributions" only if they are "earmarked" for political purposes. The regulation also provides that a payment is "earmarked" only if, at the time the payment is made, the donor knows or has reason to know that the payment or funds with which it will
